cases.title: Acute Scrotal Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: 6-hour history of sudden severe left testicular pain

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

questions.stem: A patient has the following ABG: pH 7.28, pCO2 24 mmHg, HCO3 11 mEq/L. Serum Na 140, Cl 100, K 4.0, albumin 4.0. What is the acid-base interpretation?

options.text: Non-anion gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA) with appropriate respiratory compensation
options.explanation: Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 140 - (100 + 11) = 29. This is elevated (normal 12 ± 4), indicating an anion gap metabolic acidosis, not NAGMA.

options.text: Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
options.explanation: AG = 140 - (100+11) = 29 (elevated). Expected pCO2 by Winter's formula = 1.5(11) + 8 ± 2 = 22.5-26.5. Actual pCO2 of 24 is within this range, confirming appropriate respiratory compensation. Calculate delta-delta: Δ AG = 29-12 = 17; Δ HCO3 = 24-11 = 13. Ratio ~1.3 suggests pure AGMA without concurrent non-AG acidosis or metabolic alkalosis.

options.text: Mixed anion gap metabolic acidosis and respiratory alkalosis
options.explanation: The pCO2 of 24 falls within Winter's formula range (22.5-26.5), indicating appropriate compensation rather than a primary respiratory alkalosis.

options.text: Anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent non-anion gap metabolic acidosis
options.explanation: Delta-delta ratio is ~1.3 (17/13), which falls in the 1-2 range indicating pure AGMA. A ratio <1 would suggest concurrent NAGMA.

questions.stem: A newborn pulse oximetry screen shows 88% on the right hand and 95% on the left foot. What does this suggest?

options.text: Reverse differential cyanosis — suggests transposition of the great arteries with coarctation
options.explanation: Normally, right hand (preductal) SpO2 should be HIGHER than foot (postductal). Here it's reversed: foot > right hand. This 'reverse differential cyanosis' occurs when oxygenated blood from a PDA preferentially supplies the descending aorta while deoxygenated blood goes to the upper body — classic for TGA with coarctation or interrupted aortic arch.

options.text: Normal variant — up to 10% difference between extremities is acceptable
options.explanation: A >3% difference is abnormal on CCHD screening. Moreover, the right hand being LOWER than the foot is never normal and suggests specific cardiac pathology.

options.text: Standard differential cyanosis from a large PDA with left-to-right shunt
options.explanation: Standard differential cyanosis (lower SpO2 in feet than right hand) occurs with right-to-left PDA shunting. Here the pattern is REVERSED (right hand lower), which has a different differential.

options.text: Equipment malfunction — repeat testing on the same extremity
options.explanation: While equipment error should be considered, a specific pattern (reverse differential) with both readings in clinically relevant ranges warrants urgent cardiac evaluation, not dismissal.
